topic: A person finds out their surprise party has been cancelled
raw_output:
[story 1]

The silence in the apartment was deafening, punctuated only by the faint hum of the refrigerator. I stared at the unopened invitation, the glossy card feeling suddenly heavy and mocking in my hand. My palms were slick, and I kept rubbing them together, a nervous, repetitive motion. I re-read the hastily scribbled note taped to the fridge: "Party cancelled. So sorry!" A slow, hot flush crept up my neck and flooded my cheeks. I quickly shoved the card into my pocket, desperate to hide the evidence of my disappointment. Every shadow in the room seemed to watch me, and I felt an overwhelming urge to shrink into the worn armchair, wishing the floor would swallow me whole.

[story 2]

"No, seriously?" I managed, the words coming out thin and reedy, barely audible above the traffic outside. I watched Sarah laugh with her friends, the sound sharp and bright, and the contrast was a physical blow. My stomach twisted into a tight knot, and I instinctively smoothed down my shirt, checking the collar as if a loose thread might betray my inner turmoil. I avoided eye contact, focusing instead on the pattern of the rug, tracing the fibers with my gaze. A deep, hollow ache settled in my chest, making every breath feel shallow and inadequate. I wanted to disappear, to become invisible until this awkward moment passed.

[story 3]

I paced the length of the living room three times, my movements jerky and undirected. Each footfall seemed amplified in the quiet space, echoing the frantic thumping in my chest. When I finally looked down at the message on my phone screen—the cancellation notice—a wave of intense heat washed over me, making my skin prickle. My jaw tightened, and I bit down hard on my lower lip until I tasted copper. I turned away from the window, focusing intently on a chipped ceramic vase, trying to anchor myself to something solid. The urge to simply retreat, to become utterly unremarkable, was almost overpowering.

I paced the length of the living room three times, my movements jerky and undirected. Each footfall seemed amplified in the quiet space, echoing the frantic thumping in my chest. The urge to simply retreat, to become utterly unremarkable, was almost overpowering.
